title: Whether old letters should be reread or burned
summary: Argue whether keeping and rereading personal correspondence from the past serves people well, or whether destruction is the wiser choice.
topic: Whether old letters should be reread or burned
prompt: Write an argumentative essay (350–500 words) on whether people should keep and reread old personal letters and messages, or whether destroying them is the wiser choice. Consider what old correspondence does to memory, identity, and relationships. You may refer to real examples (e.g. Kafka's instruction to Max Brod, Emily Dickinson's letters, or the destruction of correspondence by public figures). Use a cleft sentence, a concession, and at least one passive construction. Take a clear position.
hintStarters: Franz Kafka asked his closest friend Max Brod to burn everything he had ever written…
What a reread letter forces on the reader is not fresh information but an encounter with…
Granted, there is something to be said for the archive: letters have been used by historians to…
The past, once set down on paper, does not stay fixed — it shifts every time…

hintVocab.meaningVi: thư từ / sự liên lạc bằng văn bản
không thể thu hồi / không thể đảo ngược
sự thanh lọc tâm hồn / sự giải phóng cảm xúc
làm méo mó cảm giác về
bị cầm tù bởi / bị chi phối bởi
đối mặt với bản thân trong quá khứ
ngôn từ viết ra / văn bản

sampleText:
Franz Kafka asked his closest friend Max Brod to burn everything he had ever written. Brod refused. The result was The Trial, The Castle, and a literary estate that has been argued over in courts for a century. Most of us will not face this particular dilemma, but the question Kafka posed — what should be done with the written record of a private life? — is one that arrives, eventually, for almost everyone who has kept letters.

What a reread letter forces on the reader is not fresh information but an unexpected confrontation with a former self. The person who wrote with such certainty about a relationship, a plan, or a belief is recognisably you — but the gap between that certainty and your current view can be disorienting. Rereading can sharpen understanding: letters from a difficult period can clarify why things went wrong, and that clarity is sometimes worth the discomfort. Historians and biographers have long argued that personal correspondence is irreplaceable evidence, and the destruction of letters by public figures has repeatedly been lamented as a loss to the historical record.

Granted, not all letters were written to survive their moment. A message dashed off in anger, an apology that was never acknowledged, an expression of feeling that was never returned — these were not composed for rereading; they were composed for sending. To treat them as permanent documents is to misread their original purpose. There is something to be said for the decision to let emotional residue be cleared away rather than preserved, and many people report a genuine sense of catharsis after destroying old correspondence that no longer serves them.

Even so, the case for preservation is stronger than it looks. Burning a letter is irrevocable in a way that simply not rereading it is not. A box of old correspondence tucked under a bed can remain unread for years and still be available if it is needed — to settle a dispute, to reconstruct a shared past, or simply to read at a moment when the reader is ready. What is lost when letters are destroyed is not only the past they describe but the possibility of future understanding.

The wiser course is not to burn but to choose when to read. Old letters are not obligations. They can be kept without being opened, and they can be held without being allowed to hold the reader captive. The past does not have to be revisited to be preserved — and preservation, unlike destruction, leaves options open.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: 'What' cleft to name what rereading actually does
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'What a reread letter forces on the reader is not fresh information but an unexpected confrontation with a former self' dùng cấu trúc 'what X does is Y' để tập trung vào hiệu ứng thực sự của hành động (đối mặt với bản thân trong quá khứ), thay vì cái mà người đọc có thể kỳ vọng (thông tin mới).
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'What a reread letter forces on the reader is not fresh information but an unexpected confrontation' uses a what-cleft to replace the expected effect (new information) with the real one (self-confrontation), creating a sharp pivot in the essay's argument.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Passive to distribute accountability widely
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'the destruction of letters by public figures has repeatedly been lamented as a loss to the historical record' dùng bị động ('has been lamented') để ám chỉ rằng sự tiếc nuối này là phổ quát — không chỉ một sử gia hay một người chỉ trích — và cụm 'by public figures' được dịch chuyển để tập trung vào hành động tiêu hủy, không phải kẻ thực hiện.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'has repeatedly been lamented' is a passive construction in the present perfect that signals a broad, ongoing consensus rather than one person's opinion, strengthening the claim's authority.

sampleTranslationVi:
Franz Kafka đã yêu cầu người bạn thân nhất của mình, Max Brod, đốt tất cả những gì ông từng viết. Brod từ chối. Kết quả là Vụ Án, Lâu Đài, và một di sản văn học đã bị tranh chấp tại tòa án trong suốt một thế kỷ. Hầu hết chúng ta sẽ không đối mặt với tình huống đặc biệt này, nhưng câu hỏi mà Kafka đặt ra — phải làm gì với hồ sơ viết tay về một cuộc đời riêng tư? — là câu hỏi đến với hầu như tất cả mọi người đã từng giữ thư từ.

Điều mà một bức thư được đọc lại buộc người đọc phải đối mặt không phải là thông tin mới mà là sự đối đầu bất ngờ với bản thân trong quá khứ. Người đã viết với sự chắc chắn như vậy về một mối quan hệ, một kế hoạch, hay một niềm tin là bạn — nhưng khoảng cách giữa sự chắc chắn đó và quan điểm hiện tại của bạn có thể gây mất phương hướng. Đọc lại có thể làm sắc nét sự hiểu biết: những bức thư từ một giai đoạn khó khăn có thể làm rõ tại sao mọi thứ đã đi sai hướng, và sự rõ ràng đó đôi khi đáng với sự khó chịu. Các nhà sử học và nhà viết tiểu sử từ lâu đã lập luận rằng thư từ cá nhân là bằng chứng không thể thay thế, và việc tiêu hủy thư từ của các nhân vật công chúng đã bị than thở nhiều lần như một tổn thất đối với hồ sơ lịch sử.

Công nhận, không phải tất cả các bức thư đều được viết để tồn tại qua thời điểm của chúng. Một tin nhắn viết vội trong lúc giận dữ, một lời xin lỗi không bao giờ được công nhận, một sự bày tỏ cảm xúc không bao giờ được đáp lại — những thứ này không được viết để đọc lại; chúng được viết để gửi đi. Đối xử với chúng như các tài liệu vĩnh cửu là hiểu sai mục đích ban đầu của chúng. Có điều gì đó đáng để nói về quyết định để tàn dư cảm xúc được dọn sạch thay vì được giữ lại, và nhiều người báo cáo cảm giác thanh lọc thực sự sau khi tiêu hủy thư từ cũ không còn phục vụ họ nữa.

Dẫu vậy, lập luận cho việc bảo tồn mạnh hơn vẻ ngoài của nó. Đốt một bức thư là không thể thu hồi theo cách mà đơn giản là không đọc lại thì không phải vậy. Một hộp thư từ cũ được giấu dưới gầm giường có thể không được đọc trong nhiều năm và vẫn có thể được sử dụng nếu cần — để giải quyết tranh chấp, tái dựng quá khứ chung, hoặc đơn giản là đọc vào thời điểm người đọc đã sẵn sàng. Điều bị mất khi thư từ bị phá hủy không chỉ là quá khứ mà chúng mô tả mà còn là khả năng hiểu biết trong tương lai.

Lựa chọn khôn ngoan hơn không phải là đốt mà là chọn khi nào đọc. Thư từ cũ không phải là nghĩa vụ. Chúng có thể được giữ mà không cần mở ra, và chúng có thể được giữ mà không để nó cầm tù người đọc. Quá khứ không nhất thiết phải được xem lại để được bảo tồn — và bảo tồn, không giống như hủy hoại, để lại các lựa chọn mở.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a 'what' cleft to describe the real effect of rereading?
options: Rereading old letters provides fresh information about what happened.
What a reread letter forces on the reader is not fresh information but an unexpected confrontation with a former self.
It is the reader who forces themselves to confront what the letter says.
What the reader finds is that old letters always contain fresh information.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'What X forces on Y is not A but B' uses a cleft to contrast the expected effect (fresh information) with the real one (self-confrontation).
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the structure 'What X does is not A but B'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a passive correctly to suggest that destruction of letters is widely lamented?
options: Many historians lament that public figures destroy their letters.
the destruction of letters by public figures has repeatedly been lamented as a loss to the historical record
Public figures have been lamented for having repeatedly destroyed their letters.
Letters destroyed by public figures are lamented by historians repeatedly.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'has repeatedly been lamented' places the destruction in subject position and implies broad, ongoing consensus without naming a specific lamenter.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option keeps 'destruction' as the subject and uses passive to suggest widespread regret?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'irrevocable' mean in this essay?
options: Reversible with effort
Impossible to undo or take back
Legally binding
Emotionally difficult
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Irrevocable' means that something cannot be undone — once a letter is burned, it is gone permanently.
mcQuestions.hint: The prefix 'ir-' means 'not', and 'revoke' means 'take back'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which is the correct meaning of 'dashed off' in 'a message dashed off in anger'?
options: Written carefully over several days
Sent by a professional courier
Written quickly without much thought
Deleted before it could be sent
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Dash off' means to write or produce something quickly and without much care — the phrase implies impulsiveness.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which concession opener is used in the essay to introduce the opposing view?
options: Even so,
By contrast,
Granted, not all letters were written to survive their moment.
On the other hand, burning letters is common.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Granted, …' is the concession opener used. It admits a point in favour of the opposing view (that some letters were never meant to last) before the essay pivots back.
mcQuestions.hint: Find the one-word opener that signals a concession.

mcQuestions.prompt: What is 'catharsis' in the context of destroying old letters?
options: A legal process for reclaiming property
A sense of emotional release and relief
A form of historical documentation
A psychological disorder related to hoarding
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Catharsis' is the feeling of emotional release or purging, often experienced after confronting or eliminating something emotionally burdensome.
mcQuestions.hint: Catharsis is what you feel after releasing pent-up emotion.

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the essay's main argument?
options: All letters should be burned immediately after being read.
Old letters should always be kept and reread regularly.
It is wiser to keep letters without being obliged to reread them than to destroy them.
Personal correspondence has no value after the relationship it describes has ended.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay concludes that preservation is preferable to destruction because it keeps options open, while the choice of when to read remains with the reader.
mcQuestions.hint: The essay's final paragraph gives its clearest statement of position.

mcQuestions.prompt: What reason do historians give for keeping personal correspondence?
options: Letters can be sold to raise funds for literary estates.
Personal correspondence is irreplaceable evidence for reconstructing history.
Letters prove that the writer had good intentions.
Correspondence prevents future disputes between family members.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay states that historians and biographers argue personal correspondence is 'irreplaceable evidence' — it reveals things no official document records.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for what historians call correspondence in the second paragraph.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which phrase means 'to be controlled or limited by something from the past'?
options: to be haunted by
to be held hostage to
to be confronted with
to be tucked under
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Held hostage to' implies that the past has a coercive grip on the present — the person cannot move freely because they are controlled by something they cannot release.
mcQuestions.hint: A hostage cannot leave — which phrase uses this image?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which phrase best signals a pivot from concession back to the main argument?
options: In conclusion,
For instance,
Even so, the case for preservation is stronger than it looks.
In today's world,
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Even so, …' acknowledges what was just conceded and pivots back to the writer's main position — a standard B2+ move.
mcQuestions.hint: Which phrase directly follows the concession paragraph and redirects the argument?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses a participle clause to add concise context?
options: A box of old correspondence that it is tucked under a bed can remain unread for years.
A box of old correspondence tucked under a bed can remain unread for years and still be available if it is needed.
A box of old correspondence, that is tucked under a bed, can remain for years unread.
A box of old correspondence to be tucked under a bed remains unread for years.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'tucked under a bed' is a participial phrase that modifies the noun economically without a full relative clause.
mcQuestions.hint: A past participle after a noun can replace 'which is/was' — no relative pronoun needed.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which word means 'a feeling of lingering emotion left behind by a past experience'?
options: nostalgia
catharsis
emotional residue
archival value
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Emotional residue' means the traces or remnants of feeling that remain after an experience has passed — like sediment left by water.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Residue' is what remains after the main substance has gone.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which of these correctly contrasts two options using 'not … but …'?
options: these were not composed for rereading; they were composed for sending
these were not composed but for rereading they were composed for sending
these were not composed for rereading or sending they were composed for
for rereading they were not composed, for sending not either
mcQuestions.explanation: The contrast 'not for rereading … for sending' is made clearest by the parallel semicolon structure: 'not X; [they were] Y'.
mcQuestions.hint: The clearest contrast uses parallel grammar on both sides.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the Kafka anecdote demonstrate at the start of the essay?
options: That authors should always destroy their unfinished work
That the question of what to do with private writing is not trivial — even great work has been at stake
That Max Brod was wrong to disobey Kafka's wishes
That literary estates should be managed by governments, not friends
mcQuestions.explanation: The Kafka anecdote opens with a famous, high-stakes case that shows the question of destroying versus preserving writing is serious and unresolved, even at the level of major literature.
mcQuestions.hint: Think about why the essay opens with this story — what does it establish?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option maintains a consistent B2 formal register?
options: Old letters r kinda cringe tbh, just delete them.
The past does not have to be revisited to be preserved — and preservation, unlike destruction, leaves options open.
Everyone knows that burning letters is always a bad idea for sure.
Letters are important and should be kept always because you never know.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B uses precise parallelism, a formal contrast, and an aphoristic closing that is appropriate for B2 analytical writing.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for precise vocabulary, controlled tone, and no colloquialisms.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'to settle' in the context of resolving something?
options: Old letters can be used to settle a dispute.
Old letters can settle on a dispute.
Old letters settle into a dispute.
Old letters can be settled by a dispute.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Settle a dispute' is the correct collocation — 'settle' here means to resolve or bring to a conclusion.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Settle a dispute' = resolve a disagreement. Check the preposition.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Do you have old messages, letters, or diary entries that you have never deleted or thrown away? What has stopped you? If you reread them now, do you think your reaction would be relief, regret, or something else?
